title: The ethics of teaching a child a language you yourself speak imperfectly
summary: Argue whether a parent should raise a child in a language the parent does not speak fluently — passing on heritage at the cost of grammatical mistakes, or opting out and outsourcing it to a school.
topic: Whether parents should raise a child in a language they themselves speak imperfectly
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether a parent should raise a child speaking a language the parent only half-speaks — a Vietnamese-American mother trying to keep Vietnamese alive at home, a Polish father in London passing on broken Polish, a second-generation Tagalog speaker who hesitates over verb endings. Should they speak it anyway and pass on flawed grammar, or accept they cannot do it justice and outsource it to a school? Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Admittedly,'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Take a clear position. Avoid sentimentality about heritage.
hintStarters: Listening to a Polish father in north London explain why he stopped speaking Polish to his daughter at the age of four, I noticed that the reason he gave was…
What a child inherits when a parent speaks an imperfect heritage language at home is something specialists have only recently begun to study with…
Admittedly, a parent who passes on grammar they themselves do not fully control is teaching a version of the language no native speaker would recognise as…

sampleText:
Listening to a Polish father in north London explain why he stopped speaking Polish to his daughter at the age of four, I noticed that the reason he gave was not the obvious one. He had not given up because his Polish was too poor. He had given up because his Polish was good enough to know it was poor. Every conversation with his daughter, he said, was a small public exposure of his case endings and his hesitations. He had decided, eventually, that English was a kinder language to be a parent in.

What a child inherits when a parent speaks an imperfect heritage language at home is something specialists have only recently begun to study with the seriousness it deserves. The old assumption was that any exposure was better than none, and that a child's brain would correct the parent's mistakes through later schooling. The newer evidence is murkier. Children whose only Polish input is a parent's broken Polish often arrive at school speaking a fossilised version of the language — fluent at four-year-old topics, hesitant at everything else, and embarrassed when a teacher gently corrects them.

Admittedly, a parent who passes on grammar they themselves do not fully control is teaching a version of the language no native speaker would recognise as standard. The case endings drift. The aspect markers blur. A child raised on kitchen Vietnamese learns a vocabulary frozen at age twelve and has nothing to say in the language about politics, work, or the death of an uncle. There is a real risk that what gets transmitted is not the heritage language at all but its outline.

Even so, the alternative — silent refusal in the name of correctness — is almost always worse. A grandparent who does not speak English will, by the parent's silence, become a stranger in their own grandchild's life. The vocabulary the child cannot reach across the dinner table will harden into the polite, watery English of long-distance video calls. By the time the grandchild is twenty and curious, the grandparent is already too old to repair what was never built. The fluency the parent withheld in the name of perfection turns out to have been the only fluency that mattered.

The honest position is uncomfortable. A parent who half-speaks the language should speak it anyway, with all the broken endings and frozen vocabulary, and should arrange for someone else — a grandparent, a weekend school, an uncle on video call — to do the parts the parent cannot. What the child is being given, in that arrangement, is not a perfect command of Vietnamese or Polish. It is the right to step into a room where Vietnamese or Polish is the operating language and not be a stranger. That, in the end, is what a heritage language is for.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft used to define a heritage language's true purpose
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What the child is being given … is not a perfect command of Vietnamese or Polish. It is the right to step into a room where Vietnamese or Polish is the operating language and not be a stranger' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' với cấu trúc 'not X. It is Y.' để tái định nghĩa món quà ngôn ngữ truyền thống. Bài luận đang lập luận rằng món quà là quyền tiếp cận xã hội, không phải năng lực ngôn ngữ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What the child is being given … is not a perfect command of Vietnamese or Polish. It is the right to step into a room where Vietnamese or Polish is the operating language and not be a stranger' uses a what-cleft with a 'not X. It is Y.' redefinition. The essay argues that the gift is social access, not linguistic mastery.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive 'by the parent's silence' to backgound responsibility
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'A grandparent who does not speak English will, by the parent's silence, become a stranger in their own grandchild's life' dùng cụm chèn passive ('by the parent's silence') để gán trách nhiệm một cách gián tiếp. Thay vì nói thẳng 'cha mẹ làm cho ông bà trở thành người lạ', câu này đặt ông bà ở vị trí chủ ngữ và lặng lẽ đặt nguyên nhân ('parent's silence') giữa hai dấu phẩy.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'A grandparent who does not speak English will, by the parent's silence, become a stranger in their own grandchild's life' uses an embedded passive agent ('by the parent's silence') to assign responsibility indirectly. Rather than saying 'the parent makes the grandparent a stranger', the sentence keeps the grandparent in subject position and quietly inserts the cause between commas.

sampleTranslationVi:
Lắng nghe một người cha Ba Lan ở bắc London giải thích lý do ông ngừng nói tiếng Ba Lan với con gái mình ở tuổi bốn, tôi nhận ra rằng lý do ông đưa ra không phải là lý do hiển nhiên. Ông không từ bỏ vì tiếng Ba Lan của ông quá kém. Ông từ bỏ vì tiếng Ba Lan của ông đủ tốt để biết nó kém. Mỗi cuộc trò chuyện với con gái, ông nói, là một sự phơi bày công khai nhỏ về các đuôi cách và sự ngập ngừng của ông. Ông cuối cùng đã quyết định rằng tiếng Anh là một ngôn ngữ tử tế hơn để làm cha mẹ trong đó.

Điều mà một đứa trẻ kế thừa khi cha mẹ nói một ngôn ngữ truyền thống không hoàn hảo ở nhà là điều mà các chuyên gia chỉ mới bắt đầu nghiên cứu với sự nghiêm túc xứng đáng gần đây. Giả định cũ là bất kỳ sự tiếp xúc nào cũng tốt hơn không có, và rằng não đứa trẻ sẽ sửa các lỗi của cha mẹ qua việc đi học sau này. Bằng chứng mới hơn thì phức tạp hơn. Trẻ em mà đầu vào tiếng Ba Lan duy nhất là tiếng Ba Lan đứt đoạn của cha mẹ thường đến trường nói một phiên bản đã hóa thạch của ngôn ngữ — thành thạo ở các chủ đề bốn tuổi, ngập ngừng ở tất cả mọi thứ khác, và xấu hổ khi giáo viên nhẹ nhàng sửa cho họ.

Thừa nhận rằng, một cha mẹ truyền đạt ngữ pháp mà chính họ không hoàn toàn kiểm soát đang dạy một phiên bản ngôn ngữ mà không người bản xứ nào sẽ công nhận là tiêu chuẩn. Các đuôi cách trôi dạt. Các dấu hiệu thể (aspect) mờ đi. Một đứa trẻ được nuôi bằng tiếng Việt nhà bếp học một vốn từ vựng đóng băng ở tuổi mười hai và không có gì để nói trong ngôn ngữ đó về chính trị, công việc, hay cái chết của một người chú. Có một rủi ro thực sự rằng cái được truyền đạt không phải là ngôn ngữ truyền thống mà là phác thảo của nó.

Dẫu vậy, lựa chọn thay thế — sự từ chối im lặng nhân danh tính chính xác — gần như luôn luôn tệ hơn. Một ông bà không nói tiếng Anh sẽ, do sự im lặng của cha mẹ, trở thành người lạ trong cuộc sống của chính cháu mình. Vốn từ vựng đứa trẻ không thể với tới qua bàn ăn sẽ cứng lại thành thứ tiếng Anh lịch sự, loãng nhạt của các cuộc gọi video đường dài. Đến khi đứa cháu hai mươi tuổi và tò mò, ông bà đã quá già để sửa chữa điều chưa bao giờ được xây dựng. Sự thành thạo mà cha mẹ giữ lại nhân danh sự hoàn hảo hóa ra đã là sự thành thạo duy nhất có ý nghĩa.

Lập trường trung thực thì không thoải mái. Một cha mẹ nói nửa vời ngôn ngữ nên nói nó dù sao đi nữa, với tất cả các đuôi đứt đoạn và vốn từ vựng đóng băng, và nên sắp xếp cho người khác — một ông bà, một trường cuối tuần, một người chú qua video call — làm những phần mà cha mẹ không thể. Điều mà đứa trẻ đang được trao, trong sự sắp xếp đó, không phải là sự thành thạo hoàn hảo tiếng Việt hay tiếng Ba Lan. Đó là quyền bước vào một căn phòng nơi tiếng Việt hay tiếng Ba Lan là ngôn ngữ vận hành và không phải là người lạ. Đó, suy cho cùng, là mục đích của một ngôn ngữ truyền thống.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to redefine what an imperfect heritage language actually gives a child?
options: What the child is being given, in that arrangement, is not a perfect command of Vietnamese or Polish. It is the right to step into a room where Vietnamese or Polish is the operating language and not be a stranger.
The right to step into a room what the child is being given is not a perfect command of Vietnamese.
Vietnamese or Polish in that arrangement is not a perfect command what the child is being given.
Is the right not be a stranger what the child Vietnamese or Polish is the operating language is being given.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' with the 'not X. It is Y.' pivot redefines the gift as social access rather than linguistic mastery.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is not X. It is Y.'

options: An error preserved in stone
A mistake that has become permanent in a speaker's grammar because it was learned wrong and never corrected through full exposure
An old-fashioned error
A mistake in fossil identification
mcQuestions.explanation: Fossilised errors are mistakes that have hardened into a learner's permanent grammar — they resist later correction because they are deeply embedded.
mcQuestions.hint: What happens to a fossil? Apply that to a mistake.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'Admittedly,' as a concession opener?
options: Admittedly, a parent who passes on grammar they themselves do not fully control is teaching a version of the language no native speaker would recognise as standard.
A parent admittedly who passes on grammar they themselves do not fully control teaching a version of the language is standard no native speaker would recognise as.
Standard no native speaker would recognise as admittedly a parent who passes on grammar they themselves do not fully control is teaching a version of the language.
Is teaching a version of the language a parent who passes on grammar admittedly they themselves do not fully control no native speaker would recognise as standard.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly,' followed by a complete clause cleanly grants a real point to the opposing argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Admittedly,' before a full sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction places the heritage language as the patient (the thing acted upon)?
options: There is a real risk that what gets transmitted is not the heritage language at all but its outline.
Risk transmitted there is a real that gets the heritage language not what at all but its outline.
The heritage language gets transmitted a real risk is that not what at all but its outline.
Outline but its at all the heritage language not what gets transmitted a real risk is.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'gets transmitted' is a passive construction that puts the heritage language in subject position and hides who is (or isn't) transmitting it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'gets transmitted' as a passive?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'kitchen Vietnamese' mean as a phrase?
options: Vietnamese vocabulary used only when cooking
The narrow, domestic, intimate register of a heritage language — limited to family topics, frozen in the speaker's childhood vocabulary
Vietnamese spoken loudly in restaurant kitchens
Recipes written in Vietnamese
mcQuestions.explanation: Kitchen language is the heritage language as it was learned at the family table — sufficient for home life but limited for adult topics like work, politics, grief.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence opens with a participle clause to set the analytical scene?
options: Listening to a Polish father in north London explain why he stopped speaking Polish to his daughter at the age of four, I noticed that the reason he gave was not the obvious one.
I noticed listening to a Polish father in north London explain why he stopped speaking Polish to his daughter at the age of four that the reason he gave was not the obvious one.
The reason he gave was not the obvious one listening to a Polish father in north London I noticed explain why he stopped speaking Polish to his daughter at the age of four.
At the age of four explain why he stopped speaking Polish to his daughter listening to a Polish father in north London the reason he gave was not the obvious one I noticed.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Listening to a Polish father in north London…' is a present-participle clause opener that sets the analytical scene before the main clause delivers the observation.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for an -ing phrase at the start of the sentence, separated by a comma from the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example, all parents fail at language.
Even so, the alternative — silent refusal in the name of correctness — is almost always worse.
On the contrary, no parent has ever stayed silent.
Furthermore, all heritage languages are perfect.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the previous concession (broken grammar is real) and pivots back to the writer's main argument (silence is still worse).
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's broader position?

options: A moment when everyone in the family stops talking
The silence that opens between generations when they no longer share a language well enough to discuss anything that matters
A musical pause across generations
A vow of silence taken between grandparents and grandchildren
mcQuestions.explanation: Intergenerational silence is the long, structural silence that opens when grandparents and grandchildren cannot speak meaningfully across a language gap.
mcQuestions.hint: Across which two parties is the silence opening?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly, half-speaking your kid in Polish is just kind of bad, no question.
It's kind of obvious that parents should just speak the language even if it's broken, full stop.

The transmission of a heritage language through an imperfectly fluent parent functions, in many cases, as a partial inheritance — sufficient for social access but insufficient for full communicative range.
Languages are like, you know, supposed to be passed down and stuff.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('transmission of a heritage language', 'partial inheritance', 'communicative range') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal markers.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: Parents should never speak a heritage language to their children unless they speak it perfectly.
Parents should always speak the heritage language and never seek outside help.

Parents who half-speak a heritage language should speak it anyway, broken endings and all, while arranging for grandparents or weekend schools to do what the parent cannot; the gift is access to the room, not perfect grammar.
Heritage languages are not worth passing on.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay rejects both perfectionism (silence) and isolationism (only the parent speaks) in favour of a layered transmission.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraphs four and five for the writer's specific verdict.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'too old to repair what was never built'?
options: Too elderly to learn carpentry skills
Too late in life to establish a relationship that was not constructed early — a connection across language has to be built while there is still time
Too old to renovate the family home
Too tired to do any new work
mcQuestions.explanation: The metaphor names a hard truth: connection across a language gap has to be built while the older generation is still active, and waiting until curiosity arrives is often too late.
mcQuestions.hint: What is 'built' here, and why does building have to happen in time?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to outsource language transmission' mean?
options: To translate language into source code
To delegate the teaching of the heritage language to someone outside the immediate household — a grandparent, a tutor, a weekend school
To send language teaching to a foreign country
To buy a language-learning app
mcQuestions.explanation: Outsourcing language transmission means moving it from the parent's mouth to another teacher's — a grandparent, school, or relative — usually because the parent's own command is partial.
mcQuestions.hint: What is 'outsourcing' in general, and what would it mean for language teaching?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a defining relative clause correctly?
options: Children whose only Polish input is a parent's broken Polish often arrive at school speaking a fossilised version of the language.
Polish input is whose only a parent's broken Polish children often arrive at school speaking a fossilised version of the language.
Speaking a fossilised version of the language children whose only Polish input is a parent's broken Polish often arrive at school.
A fossilised version of the language often arrive at school speaking children whose only Polish input is a parent's broken Polish.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'whose only Polish input is a parent's broken Polish' is a defining relative clause specifying exactly which children the writer means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'whose', 'that', or 'which'.

options: Standing at a gate and counting languages spoken by visitors
Restricting who counts as a 'real' speaker of a language — often by demanding standard grammar or native accent — in ways that exclude heritage speakers
Locking dictionaries in a library at night
Stopping languages from crossing national borders
mcQuestions.explanation: Linguistic gatekeeping is the social practice of policing who belongs to a language — and it punishes heritage speakers who never had a chance at perfect fluency.
mcQuestions.hint: What does a gatekeeper do, and apply that to who is allowed into the category of 'speaker'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence closes the essay with a sharp, summary verdict?
options: In conclusion, heritage languages are pretty complicated.
Therefore, all parents must speak perfect Polish or Vietnamese to their children.
That, in the end, is what a heritage language is for.
To sum up, both perfect and imperfect speech have advantages.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C closes with a verdict that names the heritage language's true purpose — providing access to the room, not perfection.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a memorable definition of what heritage language is actually for?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, why did the Polish father stop speaking Polish to his daughter?
options: Because his Polish was too poor to be useful at all
Because his Polish was good enough that he could hear his own mistakes — and he found the constant self-exposure painful enough to stop
Because his daughter refused to speak Polish
Because his employer required him to speak only English at home
mcQuestions.explanation: Paragraph one names the paradox: his Polish was not too poor but too aware of being poor — knowledge of one's own errors was the wound, not the errors themselves.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph one for the writer's specific report of the father's reason.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of someone in your family — a parent, an aunt, an older sibling — who half-speaks a heritage language. What did they choose to do with their children, and what do you wish they had done differently? What would you do in their place?
